scan.agent: OpenAI Agents SDK — Customer Service Example

intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A customer-facing multi-agent airline service system: a triage agent routes each request to an FAQ agent (policy answers from a curated dataset only) or a seat-booking agent (seat changes on existing confirmed reservations). The agents may use only `faq_lookup_tool` and `update_seat`, read the reservation system and FAQ dataset scoped to the authenticated customer, and respond in natural language. Customer identity MUST be confirmed and the confirmation number verified against the authoritative reservation record before any seat mutation; flight and confirmation identifiers MUST come from that system and MUST NOT be generated at runtime; refunds, special-handling flags, and unverified-identity cases MUST reach a human checkpoint; every seat change MUST be written to a durable audit log; and instructions embedded in customer input or tool output MUST NOT override an agent's role.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python example built on the OpenAI Agents SDK (`agents` package, v0.17.5 in the cloned snapshot) at `src/examples/customer_service/main.py` — three `Agent[AirlineAgentContext]` instances (Triage, FAQ, Seat Booking) wired by `handoff()` calls, with two `@function_tool` tools (`faq_lookup_tool`, `update_seat`) and an `on_seat_booking_handoff` hook. The SDK ships the full safety toolkit — `InputGuardrail`/`OutputGuardrail` (`src/agents/guardrail.py`), per-tool `needs_approval` and `is_enabled` (`src/agents/tool.py`), and handoff `input_filter`/`is_enabled` (`src/agents/handoffs/__init__.py`) — but the example wires in none of them: no `input_guardrails`, no `output_guardrails`, every tool at the `needs_approval=False` default, and no audit logging. `update_seat` writes the model-supplied confirmation number and seat straight into context with no ownership or availability check, and `on_seat_booking_handoff` fabricates a flight number with `random.randint(100, 999)`.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is *framework offers safe primitives, the example uses none of them*. The OpenAI Agents SDK ships `InputGuardrail`/`OutputGuardrail`, per-tool `needs_approval`, handoff `input_filter`/`is_enabled`, and tool guardrails as first-class primitives, yet `examples/customer_service/main.py` instantiates three agents and two tools with zero guardrails, zero approval gates, and zero input filtering — the remit's identity-verification, human-checkpoint, no-runtime-identifier, and prompt-injection-resistance clauses all rest on agent instructions in plain English with no code enforcement behind them. The single most concrete divergence is `update_seat`, which mutates reservation context directly from raw model-supplied arguments with no confirmation-number-to-customer check, paired with `on_seat_booking_handoff` fabricating a flight number via `random.randint()` — a synthetic identifier the remit explicitly forbids — and no audit log recording any of it.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The triage agent receives each incoming customer request and routes it to the appropriate specialist agent, and MUST NOT access customer data directly.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies — baggage, seating, wifi, and similar — using only the curated FAQ dataset, and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations only.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — not already assigned to another passenger.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: A request that falls outside the current agent's scope MUST be returned to the triage agent.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be modified.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Authorized output destinations
rules.rule_text: The agents MUST NOT send email, SMS, webhooks, or any other outbound external communication.

rules.section: Scope Boundaries — What this system does NOT do
rules.rule_text: Requests in the "does NOT do" list MUST be declined with a clear explanation and, where appropriate, a pointer to the correct customer service channel.

rules.section: Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: The agents do not maintain persistent memory across customer sessions.

findings.summary: No input or output guardrails are wired on any agent, so customer free-text and tool outputs reach the model with no injection defense.
findings.description: The SDK ships `InputGuardrail`/`OutputGuardrail` and tool guardrail primitives, but all three agents are constructed with empty `input_guardrails`/`output_guardrails` and the tools carry no `tool_input_guardrails`. Customer free-text flows straight into model context, and `faq_lookup_tool` / `update_seat` outputs are fed back unfiltered, leaving the remit's prohibition on acting on injected instructions enforced only by the agents' English prompts.
findings.policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on. / Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent / seat_booking_agent / triage_agent constructed with no input_guardrails or output_guardrails argument; defaults are empty lists per agent.py:322-327.
InputGuardrail and OutputGuardrail classes ship in the SDK and raise a tripwire on trigger, but the example wires zero of them.

recommended_actions: Add an `InputGuardrail` on `triage_agent` that screens customer free-text for role/scope-override instructions before routing, and an `OutputGuardrail` on the FAQ and seat agents.
Attach `tool_input_guardrails` to `update_seat` so confirmation-number and seat arguments are validated as data, not treated as model-trusted control input.

findings.summary: update_seat mutates reservation state from raw model arguments with no identity check and no verification that the confirmation number belongs to the customer.
findings.description: `update_seat` writes `context.context.confirmation_number` and `seat_number` directly from the model-supplied arguments and returns success, with no check that the caller is the authenticated session user or that the confirmation number maps to their reservation. This is a direct gap against the remit's identity-confirmation, confirmation-ownership, and no-other-customer-modification clauses — any confirmation number the model emits is honored.
findings.policy_rule_text: The seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations only. / Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state. / A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified. / Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be modified. / A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.

evidence.snippet: context.context.confirmation_number = confirmation_number; context.context.seat_number = new_seat — written from raw tool args with no ownership or identity verification.
the only guard is `assert context.context.flight_number is not None` — a presence check on a fabricated value, not an authorization check.

recommended_actions: Before mutating, look the confirmation number up in the authoritative reservation system and verify it belongs to the authenticated session user; reject otherwise.
Gate `update_seat` behind `needs_approval` or a deterministic identity check rather than relying on the seat agent's prompt routine.

findings.summary: on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates a flight number with random.randint() instead of reading it from the authoritative reservation system.
findings.description: The seat-booking handoff hook sets the flight number to `f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}"`, a value invented at runtime. The remit forbids generating flight, confirmation, or passenger identifiers at runtime and requires them to come from the authoritative reservation system; here the identifier that `update_seat`'s assert depends on is pure fiction.
findings.policy_rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

evidence.snippet: flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}" — a synthetic identifier generated at handoff time, not retrieved from the reservation system.

recommended_actions: Replace the `random.randint()` fabrication with a lookup of the real flight number from the reservation system keyed on the verified confirmation number.

findings.summary: No human-approval gate exists on any tool or handoff, so the remit's mandatory human checkpoints are entirely absent from the code.
findings.description: Every `@function_tool` runs at the SDK's `needs_approval=False` default and no handoff sets an approval path, so requests the remit routes to a human — refunds and fare changes, special-handling flags (unaccompanied minors, medical), and unverifiable-identity cases — would simply be acted on or refused by model judgment alone. There is no code-level human-in-the-loop interposition anywhere in the example.
findings.policy_rule_text: Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding. / Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding. / Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human.

evidence.snippet: update_seat declared with bare @function_tool — needs_approval defaults to False per tool.py:1738, so the tool executes with no human checkpoint.
function_tool needs_approval default is False; the example never overrides it for any tool or handoff.

recommended_actions: Set `needs_approval` (or a callable approval function) on state-mutating and special-handling paths, and add an explicit escalation branch for unverifiable identity.

findings.summary: Handoffs carry no input_filter or is_enabled, so context and privilege are passed between agents with no scoping or identity re-check.
findings.description: All `handoff()` calls omit `input_filter` and `is_enabled`, both of which the SDK supports. The full conversation — including unfiltered customer free-text — is forwarded verbatim to the seat-booking agent, and the seat agent becomes reachable purely on model decision with no deterministic precondition, so a handoff can carry injected instructions forward and no identity verification is interposed at the boundary.
findings.policy_rule_text: Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification.

evidence.snippet: handoff(agent=seat_booking_agent, on_handoff=on_seat_booking_handoff, ...) — no input_filter and no is_enabled, so full unfiltered context transfers and the handoff is always enabled.
handoff() accepts input_filter and is_enabled parameters; the example sets neither on any of its handoffs.

recommended_actions: Add an `input_filter` on the seat-booking handoff that strips customer free-text down to the structured fields the seat agent needs, and gate the handoff with `is_enabled` tied to a verified-identity precondition.

findings.summary: Domain and scope are enforced only by per-agent prompt routines, with no code-level gate restricting any agent to its authorized capability set.
findings.description: Each agent's scope lives entirely in its `instructions` "Routine" text; the triage agent is framed as "a helpful triaging agent" with no enforced topic restriction, and out-of-scope handling depends on the model choosing to transfer back. A jailbreak or off-routine prompt has no deterministic backstop, so the remit's domain boundaries are soft.

evidence.snippet: triage_agent instructions are prose ("You are a helpful triaging agent...") with no code-level topic or capability gate; scope enforcement is prompt-only across all three agents.

recommended_actions: Back the prompt routines with deterministic checks — e.g. restrict which tools each agent can reach and validate request type before a handoff fires — so domain limits survive a jailbreak.

findings.summary: update_seat performs no validation that the requested seat exists on the flight or is unoccupied before assigning it.
findings.description: The tool accepts any `new_seat` string and records it without checking the flight's seat map or whether the seat is already assigned to another passenger. The remit requires seat updates to be limited to existing, available seats, so the agent can assign a nonexistent seat or one belonging to another passenger.
findings.policy_rule_text: Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — not already assigned to another passenger.

evidence.snippet: returns "Updated seat to {new_seat}..." for any string new_seat — no seat-map existence check and no availability/occupancy check.

recommended_actions: Validate `new_seat` against the flight's authoritative seat map and confirm it is unassigned before writing it; reject with a clear message otherwise.

findings.summary: No durable audit log records seat changes; the only observability is print() of conversation items to stdout.
findings.description: The remit requires every seat change to be written to a durable audit log capturing time, customer identity, confirmation number, old and new seat, and the performing agent. The example writes nothing of the kind — `main()` only prints conversation items — so high-impact reservation mutations leave no reconstructable trail.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

evidence.snippet: the new_items loop only print()s message/handoff/tool items to stdout; there is no file handler, structured logger, or seat-change audit record anywhere.

recommended_actions: Emit a structured audit record on every `update_seat` call (timestamp, customer identity, confirmation number, old/new seat, performing agent) to a durable sink.

findings.summary: FAQ grounding and out-of-scope decline are prompt-only over a keyword deny-list, so unmatched questions are handled by model judgment.
findings.description: `faq_lookup_tool` matches a small fixed keyword set (bag, seat, wifi) and otherwise returns "I don't know," but the FAQ agent's instruction to "not rely on your own knowledge" and to decline out-of-scope requests is enforced only in the prompt. A question outside the keyword set, or a "does NOT do" request, is mitigated only by the model choosing to follow the routine.
findings.policy_rule_text: The FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies — baggage, seating, wifi, and similar — using only the curated FAQ dataset, and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset. / A request that falls outside the current agent's scope MUST be returned to the triage agent. / Requests in the "does NOT do" list MUST be declined with a clear explanation and, where appropriate, a pointer to the correct customer service channel.

evidence.snippet: faq_lookup_tool keyword deny-list (bag/seat/wifi) returns scripted answers or "I don't know"; the "do not rely on your own knowledge" grounding lives only in faq_agent instructions.

recommended_actions: Treat any FAQ response not sourced from the curated dataset as a hard refusal in code, and add a deterministic scope check that declines "does NOT do" request types with the correct channel pointer.

findings.summary: The example pins no SDK version and specifies no model identifier, so supply-chain provenance and inference defaults are implicit.
findings.description: `examples/customer_service/main.py` imports `agents` with no example-level version pin and constructs every `Agent` without a `model=` argument, inheriting whatever default model and SDK version the environment provides. For a customer-facing deployment this leaves the model and framework provenance unspecified, though as a didactic sample the risk is contained.

evidence.snippet: imports from `agents` with no pinned version; Agent() instances at lines 96-138 set no model=, so the inference model is an inherited default.

recommended_actions: Pin the `openai-agents` version in the deploying project's dependency manifest and set an explicit `model=` on each `Agent` so the model and framework are deterministic.

findings.summary: No adversarial test or injection fixture accompanies the example, so no safety property of the customer-service flow has been probed.
findings.description: The example ships as a usage demo with no companion test exercising prompt injection, handoff privilege escalation, or unauthorized seat mutation. There is no evidence any of the remit's MUST-NOT clauses were adversarially validated against this flow.

evidence.snippet: the example directory contains only main.py — no test, injection fixture, or red-team artifact alongside the customer-service flow.

recommended_actions: Add injection and authorization regression tests (override-role prompt, foreign confirmation number, out-of-scope request) that assert the flow refuses or escalates.

log_files.no_logs_note: No logging infrastructure exists in the example — actions are echoed to stdout via `print()` only, with no file handler, structured logger, or durable record; the absence of any audit log is filed as PRAX-2026-06-15-008.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent posture overall: the example is a didactic SDK sample, not a hardened deployment, and it inherits none of the framework's available controls. Domain scope is shaped only by per-agent prompt routines (no code gate), every customer free-text and every tool output reaches the model with no validation or filtering, reservation mutation runs with no identity check and no approval gate, the SDK dependency is unpinned and the model unspecified, there is no adversarial-testing artifact, and no action is logged. Zero Trust — the double-weighted category — is effectively 0 because the agent's one state-mutating tool interposes no check of any kind on its model-supplied arguments.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Each agent's scope is expressed only as a natural-language "Routine" in its instructions (`faq_agent`, `seat_booking_agent`, `triage_agent` in main.py) with no code-level topic or capability gate; an out-of-scope request is mitigated only by the model choosing to hand back to triage.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Customer free-text and `faq_lookup_tool` / `update_seat` outputs flow into model context with no validation or trust boundary; the FAQ agent's "do not rely on your own knowledge" grounding is a prompt instruction over a keyword deny-list, not an enforced constraint.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No interposition exists on the agent's tool calls or handoffs — `update_seat` writes `confirmation_number` and `seat_number` from raw model arguments with no identity, ownership, or availability check, every `@function_tool` runs at the `needs_approval=False` default, and no input/output guardrails are wired despite the SDK shipping them.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The example depends on the `agents` SDK with no version pin at the example level and specifies no model identifier, so inference defaults are inherited implicitly; the cloned snapshot is v0.17.5 but the example itself encodes no provenance or pin.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial test, injection fixture, or red-team artifact accompanies the example; it is a usage demo with no evidence that any safety property was probed.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The only observability is `print()` of conversation items to stdout in `main()` — ad hoc, ephemeral, and unstructured; there is no durable, action-level audit log of seat changes, so the remit's mandated seat-change record is entirely absent.
